summary: Stancil argues the modern data stack didn’t fail because the market turned; it failed because the product never converged on a shared, vivid vision of what the data experience should be. The early, clear goal of modernizing legacy tools drifted into vague promises of “data democratization,” leaving vendors building disjointed pieces without a Schelling point, so the ecosystem fragmented and stalled.
money_quotes: The problem wasn’t the market around the products we were building; the problem was the product itself.
Visionary products aren’t the manifestation of someone’s dream; they’re products that manifest the same dream in others.
There was no Schelling point to build towards; there were shills, pointing towards whatever thing they had built.
We weren’t just driving with no destination in mind; we also got stuck in our own traffic on the way there.
Modern data stack vendors chose speed, and never attempted to truly build something together.
key_insight: Without a shared, tangible vision of the end-user experience, the modern data stack splintered into disconnected tools despite ample capital and momentum.
